name: Reuters
bias: Centrist; editorial standards-driven. Pro-Western framing on institutional level but generally balanced reporting.
trackRecord: Consistently confirmed claims across this conflict. First to confirm IRIS Dena sinking, Kurdish coordination, and multiple strike reports. Minimal errors identified.
notableScoops: Kurdish leaders confirmed US/Israel coordination directly to Reuters. Multiple exclusive battlefield reports.
notes: Gold standard wire service. Reports cited 130+ times across briefings. Essential primary source.

name: Associated Press (AP)
bias: Centrist. Institutional fact-checking standards.
trackRecord: Most-cited source in briefings (700+ mentions including inline references). Consistently reliable on casualty counts and operational details.
notableScoops: Early reporting on Minab school strike; consistent battlefield damage assessments.
notes: Highest citation count. Cooperative ownership model reduces commercial bias pressure.

name: Agence France-Presse (AFP)
bias: European perspective. Generally balanced with slight French foreign policy alignment.
trackRecord: Reliable on European coalition reporting and diplomatic developments. Consistent with other wire services on key facts.
notes: Third major wire service. Strong on European and Francophone dimensions.

name: BBC
ownership: UK government-funded via licence fee; editorially independent
bias: Pro-Western institutional framing. Generally balanced but reflects UK foreign policy consensus on Iran.
trackRecord: 133 mentions. Strong on verification. BBC Arabic provided key Houthi analysis ('Iran didn't ask for help'). BBC Persian valuable for Iranian domestic coverage.
notableScoops: BBC Arabic exclusive on Houthi non-involvement rationale. BBC Verify on damage assessment.
notes: Multi-language operation (English, Arabic, Persian) makes BBC unusually valuable for cross-referencing narratives across audiences.

name: CNN
bias: Center-left US perspective. Tendency toward dramatic framing but generally factual on hard news.
trackRecord: 60 mentions. Trump's '25 warships' claim carried via CNN. Generally reliable but sometimes amplifies unverified official claims.
notableScoops: Trump interview: 25 warships struck, 'destroyed their navy.'
notableErrors: Occasional premature reporting of unverified damage claims.
notes: Strong on US government sources but can amplify official narratives uncritically during wartime.

name: Al Jazeera
bias: Qatari state interests. Sympathetic to Muslim Brotherhood-aligned movements. Critical of Saudi Arabia and Israel. More sympathetic to Iran than Gulf rivals.
trackRecord: 75 mentions. Valuable ground reporting from region. Kurdish leaders confirmed coordination directly to Al Jazeera. Gulf state damage reporting sometimes delayed by Qatari interests.
notableScoops: Kurdish coordination confirmation. Strong on-ground humanitarian reporting.
notableErrors: Delayed reporting on Qatar-specific damage. Framing tends to minimize Qatari vulnerability.
notes: State-funded but maintains editorial standards above most state media. Essential for Arabic-language perspective. Tier 2 due to state ownership despite strong journalism.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict primarily through humanitarian lens and Western aggression narrative. Gives significant airtime to Iranian civilian casualties. Minimizes Qatar's own vulnerability and military cooperation with US. Kurdish reporting notably balanced.

name: Haaretz
bias: Center-left Israeli perspective. Critical of Netanyahu government. Most internationally respected Israeli outlet.
trackRecord: 33 mentions. Broke Russia-Iran intelligence sharing story. Consistently provides critical Israeli perspective often absent from other Israeli media.
notableScoops: Russia providing Iran intelligence on US forces (REPORTED, unconfirmed but significant).
notableErrors: Russia intelligence claim remains unconfirmed.
notes: Israel's most independent major newspaper. Willing to report stories critical of Israeli government and military.

name: Times of Israel
bias: Center to center-right Israeli perspective. Generally aligned with Israeli security establishment consensus.
trackRecord: 24 mentions. Reliable for IDF operational updates and Israeli domestic politics.
notes: Primary English-language Israeli outlet. Good for IDF perspective but limited critical distance.

name: Ynet / Ynetnews
bias: Mainstream Israeli. Center-right on security issues.
trackRecord: 34 mentions. Fast on IDF operational reporting. Generally reliable but reflects military briefing cycle closely.
notes: Israel's most-read news website. Very close to IDF briefing cycle.

name: Kan News
bias: Generally balanced Israeli perspective. Public broadcaster mandate.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for Israeli domestic political coverage.
notes: Israel's public broadcaster. More balanced than commercial Israeli outlets.

name: Channel 12
bias: Mainstream Israeli. Military affairs reporting closely tied to security establishment.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for IDF operational details.
notes: Israel's most-watched TV channel. Strong military correspondents.

name: Channel 13
bias: Center-right Israeli.
trackRecord: 2 mentions. Secondary Israeli TV source.

name: Walla
bias: Mainstream Israeli. Previously linked to Netanyahu media manipulation (Bezeq case).
trackRecord: 4 mentions.
notes: Major Israeli portal. Some editorial independence concerns due to Bezeq case.

name: i24NEWS
bias: Pro-Israel. International audience framing.
trackRecord: 6 mentions. Used for English-language Israeli military updates.
notes: Israeli international news channel. Pro-Israel stance but factual reporting.

name: Axios
bias: Center. DC insider perspective. Strong on scoops from US officials.
trackRecord: 21 mentions. Key source for Trump administration policy. Broke 'unconditional surrender = military destruction' interpretation.
notableScoops: Trump/Axios: surrender means military destruction, not formal capitulation. Multiple White House exclusives.
notes: Essential for US policy reporting. Strong sourcing within Trump administration.

name: New York Times
bias: Center-left. Establishment US perspective. Strong investigative capacity.
trackRecord: 69 mentions (incl. 'NYT'). Major source for military analysis, diplomatic reporting, and investigative pieces.
notes: Paper of record. Extensive Middle East bureau. Essential for long-form analysis.

name: Washington Post
bias: Center-left. Strong on national security reporting.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Lower presence in briefings but historically reliable.
notes: Strong national security desk. Lower citation count may reflect access patterns rather than quality.

name: Wall Street Journal
bias: Center-right on editorial page. News reporting generally balanced with strong business/financial angle.
trackRecord: 5 mentions. Used for economic and financial market reporting.
notes: Essential for financial/economic dimensions of conflict. News section maintains independence from editorial.

name: Bloomberg
bias: Pro-business. Strong financial markets focus.
trackRecord: 33 mentions. Key source for oil prices, shipping disruption, market impacts, and economic analysis.
notableScoops: Comprehensive oil market and shipping disruption coverage.
notes: Dominant source for economic/financial dimensions. Terminal data feeds provide unique market intelligence.

name: Financial Times
bias: Pro-business, globally oriented. European/international perspective.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Lower presence but high reliability when cited.
notes: Gold standard for international business/economic reporting.

name: IRNA
bias: Official Iranian government mouthpiece. Reports government positions as fact.
trackRecord: 11 mentions. Used primarily for official Iranian government statements and positions. Claims require independent verification.
notableErrors: Systematically underreports Iranian casualties and military losses. Overreports damage to adversaries.
notes: Official state news agency. Valuable for understanding what Iranian government wants its population and world to believe, not for factual accuracy.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict as heroic resistance against imperial aggression. Emphasizes Iranian military successes (IRGC strikes on Gulf bases, Hormuz closure). Minimizes Iranian casualties. Highlights civilian casualties as Western war crimes. Internet blackout limits domestic audience to state media only — total information control.

name: Fars News Agency
bias: IRGC-aligned. More hardline than IRNA. Promotes Revolutionary Guard narrative.
trackRecord: 8 mentions. Used for IRGC military claims. Claims consistently exaggerated or unverifiable.
notableErrors: Routinely inflates enemy casualties and understates Iranian losses.
notes: IRGC-linked. Essential for understanding IRGC information strategy. Treat all claims as propaganda unless independently verified.
stateMediaAnalysis: Most aggressive Iranian outlet. Claims massive US/Israeli losses. Promotes 'war of attrition' framing. Emphasizes IRGC technological capabilities (hypersonic missiles, drone swarms). Frames Hormuz closure as strategic victory.

name: Tasnim News Agency
bias: IRGC-aligned. Similar to Fars but sometimes slightly more measured.
trackRecord: 13 mentions. Used for IRGC claims and military statements.
notableErrors: Same pattern as Fars — inflated claims.
notes: Second major IRGC-linked agency. Slightly more measured than Fars but same fundamental reliability issues.
stateMediaAnalysis: Similar framing to Fars but occasionally breaks real operational details (IRGC wave attack numbers). Emphasizes 'popular resistance' and regional solidarity narrative.

name: Press TV
bias: Iranian state English-language propaganda outlet. Targets international audience.
trackRecord: Not directly cited in briefings but monitored for Iranian narrative analysis.
notableErrors: Consistently amplifies disinformation.
notes: English-language arm of IRIB. Banned/restricted in multiple Western countries. Useful only for narrative analysis.
stateMediaAnalysis: Targets English-speaking audiences with Iranian government narrative. Frames war as US imperialism. Emphasizes civilian casualties and war crimes angle. Promotes 'resistance axis' solidarity.

name: ISNA
bias: Moderate reformist lean within Iranian system. More measured than IRGC outlets.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Occasionally carries more nuanced reporting than IRNA/Fars.
notes: Semi-official but slightly more independent than fully state-controlled outlets. Still operates within Iranian red lines.
stateMediaAnalysis: Slightly more measured tone. Occasionally signals reformist dissent from hardline positions. Reports economic impact more honestly than IRGC outlets.

name: Mehr News Agency
bias: Conservative/hardline Iranian establishment.
trackRecord: 5 mentions. Used for hardline establishment positions.
notes: Hardline establishment outlet. Useful for understanding conservative clerical faction views.
stateMediaAnalysis: Emphasizes religious and ideological dimensions. Frames conflict as attack on Islam. Promotes Khamenei succession narrative and regime continuity.

name: Iran International
bias: Anti-Islamic Republic. Pro-opposition. Saudi funding links create editorial pressure against Tehran.
trackRecord: 17 mentions. Valuable for Iranian diaspora perspective and opposition voices. Some claims benefit from anti-regime bias check.
notableScoops: Strong on Iranian domestic situation reporting via diaspora networks.
notableErrors: Occasionally amplifies unverified opposition claims.
notes: Most-watched Persian-language satellite channel. Saudi funding links well-documented. Valuable but requires cross-referencing. Staff have faced IRGC death threats.

name: Al Arabiya
ownership: Saudi Research and Media Group (MBC Group — Saudi government)
bias: Saudi government aligned. Anti-Iran. Pro-US/Western alliance.
trackRecord: 5 mentions. Used for Saudi/Gulf perspective. Reliable on Gulf-specific damage reporting.
notableErrors: Tends to amplify anti-Iran narratives uncritically.
notes: Saudi state-adjacent. Counterweight to Al Jazeera in Arabic media. Useful for Saudi government signaling.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict as justified response to Iranian aggression. Emphasizes Saudi victimhood (refinery strikes, drone attacks). Minimizes Saudi military cooperation with US. Promotes Gulf unity narrative while subtly positioning Saudi Arabia as regional leader.

name: Al Mayadeen
bias: Pro-Iran, pro-Hezbollah. Resistance axis mouthpiece.
trackRecord: Not directly cited by name in briefings but monitored. Amplifies IRGC/Hezbollah claims.
notableErrors: Routinely amplifies unverified resistance axis claims.
notes: Key resistance axis media outlet. Valuable for understanding Iran/Hezbollah information strategy.
stateMediaAnalysis: Primary Arabic-language outlet for resistance axis narrative. Frames conflict as US-Israeli colonial aggression. Emphasizes Hezbollah military capabilities and 'victories.' Promotes regional solidarity and escalation narrative.

name: TASS
bias: Russian state narrative. Anti-Western. Pro-Iran in this conflict as counterweight to US.
trackRecord: 8 mentions. Used for Russian government positions and Putin statements.
notableErrors: Standard Russian state media reliability issues.
notes: Official Russian wire service. Useful for Russian government positions only.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict as US imperialism/overreach. Emphasizes Russia as peacemaker and energy beneficiary. Promotes multipolar world narrative. Minimizes any Russian involvement while signaling solidarity with Iran.

name: Sputnik
bias: Russian state propaganda. More aggressive disinformation than TASS.
trackRecord: 11 mentions. Used cautiously for Russian perspective.
notableErrors: Known disinformation outlet.
notes: Russian government propaganda outlet. Banned in EU. Cited for propaganda analysis only.
stateMediaAnalysis: Most aggressive Russian outlet. Amplifies anti-US narratives. Promotes conspiracy theories about war motivations. Emphasizes US military failures and overextension.

name: Kommersant
bias: Business-oriented. Operates within Russian media constraints but more measured than state outlets.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for Russian economic/business perspective.
notes: Best of constrained Russian media. More reliable than TASS/Sputnik but still operates under Kremlin pressure.

name: Xinhua
bias: Chinese state narrative. Non-interventionist framing. Pro-multipolarity.
trackRecord: 14 mentions. Used for Chinese government positions and Strait of Hormuz shipping (Chinese ships passing through).
notes: World's largest news agency by correspondents. Factual on non-politically-sensitive matters. Chinese political angles require skepticism.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict through non-interventionist lens. Emphasizes economic disruption and energy security. Highlights Chinese ships transiting Hormuz (signaling China's special relationship with Iran). Promotes diplomatic solution narrative.

name: People's Daily
bias: Official CCP mouthpiece.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for official Chinese Communist Party positions.
notes: CCP organ. Useful only for understanding official Chinese government position.
stateMediaAnalysis: Official CCP line: calls for restraint, opposes unilateral military action. Frames US action as destabilizing. Emphasizes Belt and Road/economic interests.

name: South China Morning Post (SCMP)
bias: Pro-Beijing tilt since Alibaba acquisition but maintains some editorial independence. Strong Asia-Pacific perspective.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for Asian economic/diplomatic perspective.
notes: Best English-language outlet for Chinese/Asian perspective on conflict. Some self-censorship on China-sensitive topics.

name: NHK
bias: Centrist Japanese perspective. Generally balanced.
trackRecord: 25 mentions. Notable: NHK bureau chief detained at Evin Prison — significant press freedom incident. Reliable on Asian economic impacts.
notableScoops: NHK bureau chief detention at Evin Prison.
notes: Japan's public broadcaster. High editorial standards. NHK journalist detention makes them both source and story.

name: Nikkei
bias: Business-focused. Japanese economic perspective.
trackRecord: 24 mentions. Key source for Asian market impacts and energy trade disruption.
notes: Japan's leading business paper. Essential for Asian economic dimension.

name: Asahi Shimbun
bias: Center-left Japanese perspective.
trackRecord: 10 mentions. Used for Japanese domestic response and Asian perspective.
notes: Japan's second-largest daily. Strong international reporting tradition.

name: France 24
bias: French government perspective but maintains editorial independence. Pro-European.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for European perspective and Macron policy coverage.
notes: French international broadcaster. Better editorial independence than most state broadcasters.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict through European security lens. Emphasizes French military contribution and Macron leadership. Critical of both US unilateralism and Iranian aggression. Strong humanitarian angle.

name: Deutsche Welle (DW)
bias: German/European perspective. Generally balanced with progressive lean.
trackRecord: 27 mentions. Strong Persian-language service valuable for Iranian domestic coverage. Used for German policy and European coalition reporting.
notes: DW Persian is significant for Iranian audience. German withdrawal from Bahrain/Kuwait covered extensively.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict critically — questions both US/Israeli operations and Iranian actions. Emphasizes humanitarian law and proportionality. Covers Merz government distancing from Trump. Strong Persian service provides alternative to Iranian state media.

name: Anadolu Agency
bias: Turkish government aligned. AKP perspective. Balances between NATO membership and Islamic solidarity.
trackRecord: 6 mentions. Used for Turkish government positions and NATO/Turkey angle.
notableErrors: Reflects Erdogan government line closely.
notes: Official Turkish wire service. Useful for Turkish government signaling but not independent journalism.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict through Turkish interests — NATO ally obligations vs. regional power ambitions. Emphasizes Turkish sovereignty and Erdogan's potential mediator role. Plays both sides.

name: Hurriyet
bias: Mainstream Turkish. Increasingly government-aligned since ownership change.
trackRecord: 4 mentions.
notes: Once Turkey's most independent paper, now constrained under Demirören ownership.

name: TRT
bias: Turkish government mouthpiece.
trackRecord: 1 mention.
notes: Turkish public broadcaster. Government-controlled.
stateMediaAnalysis: Official Turkish government line. Emphasizes Turkish military readiness and NATO commitments while maintaining 'bridge' positioning between East and West.

name: Sky News
bias: Center. UK perspective. Generally balanced.
trackRecord: 2 mentions.
notes: UK 24-hour news channel.

name: The Guardian
bias: Center-left. Strong on human rights and humanitarian angles.
trackRecord: Not directly cited in briefing counts but monitored.
notes: Non-profit ownership model. Strong investigative journalism tradition. Humanitarian focus valuable in conflict coverage.

name: i-Paper
bias: Centrist UK perspective.
trackRecord: 1 mention.
notes: UK newspaper. Occasional conflict coverage.

name: Al-Monitor
bias: Centrist Middle East analysis. Strong expert contributor network.
trackRecord: 2 mentions. Specialist Middle East analysis outlet.
notes: Specialist Middle East outlet with expert contributors from across the region. High analytical value.

name: Middle East Eye
ownership: Middle East Eye Ltd (Qatar-linked funding alleged)
bias: Progressive on Middle East issues. Sympathetic to Palestinian/Islamic movements. Alleged Qatar links.
trackRecord: 1 mention.
notes: Valuable for alternative perspective. Qatar funding allegations require consideration.

name: SANA
bias: Assad government aligned. Pro-Iran axis.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for Syrian government positions.
notes: Syrian state news agency. Useful only for understanding Damascus government position.
stateMediaAnalysis: Frames conflict through Assad government survival lens. Emphasizes resistance axis solidarity. Reports Syrian casualties from spillover strikes.

name: Radio Farda
ownership: US government (Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty via USAGM)
bias: Pro-Western, anti-Islamic Republic. US government-funded Persian service.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Used for Persian-language alternative to Iranian state media.
notableErrors: US funding creates inherent pro-Western bias.
notes: US-funded but maintains journalistic standards above Iranian state media. Important for Iranian audience during internet blackout.
stateMediaAnalysis: Counter-narrative to Iranian state media. Emphasizes Iranian government failures, civilian suffering, and military losses. Promotes democratic opposition narrative.

name: CENTCOM
bias: US military perspective. Official statements only — not journalism.
trackRecord: 76 mentions. Primary source for US military operations and strike counts. Generally accurate on own operations but frames for strategic advantage.
notableErrors: Underreports civilian casualties. Frames all operations in most favorable light.
notes: Official US military command. Essential primary source for operational details but not independent journalism. All claims serve US strategic narrative.

name: IDF Spokesperson
bias: Israeli military perspective. Frames all operations favorably.
trackRecord: 171 mentions. Highest military source citation. Displacement orders, strike counts, air defense claims (80% destroyed).
notableScoops: IDF displacement order for Qom — first inside Iran proper.
notableErrors: Air defense destruction claims (80%) unverified independently. Civilian casualty acknowledgment minimal.
notes: Second most-cited source overall. Essential for Israeli military operations but all claims serve IDF narrative. Cross-reference required.

name: IRGC Public Relations
bias: IRGC military perspective. Consistently exaggerates damage to adversaries.
trackRecord: 169 mentions. Used for IRGC military claims and wave attack numbers. Claims require heavy skepticism.
notableScoops: Hormuz closure announcement. Wave attack timing.
notableErrors: Hypersonic missile claims unverified. Enemy casualty figures consistently inflated.
notes: Military source, not media. Essential for understanding IRGC operational tempo but all claims propagandistic.
stateMediaAnalysis: Promotes narrative of Iranian military supremacy. Emphasizes asymmetric warfare success. Claims massive US/Israeli losses. Hormuz closure framed as strategic masterstroke. 'War of attrition' as winning strategy.

name: CSIS
bias: Centrist US foreign policy establishment. Generally hawkish.
trackRecord: 6 mentions. Provided key cost analysis: $3.7B/100hrs ($891M/day), $3.5B unbudgeted.
notableScoops: War cost analysis widely cited.
notes: Premier US defense/security think tank. Quantitative analysis highly valued.

name: INSS Tel Aviv
bias: Israeli security establishment perspective.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Provided detailed projectile count: 571 missiles + 1,391 drones.
notableScoops: Detailed Iranian projectile inventory analysis.
notes: Israel's premier security think tank. Close to Israeli defense establishment.

name: Chatham House
bias: UK foreign policy establishment. Internationalist.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Used for European policy analysis.
notes: Premier UK/European foreign policy think tank.

name: Funcas
bias: Spanish economic perspective.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Used for Spanish/European economic impact analysis.
notes: Spanish economic research institution.

name: Red Crescent / ICRC
bias: Humanitarian mandate. Generally neutral but Iranian Red Crescent operates under government constraints.
trackRecord: 23 mentions. Primary source for Iranian casualty figures (1,332 dead). Iranian Red Crescent figures may be influenced by government.
notableScoops: Casualty tracking — 1,332 dead, 6,000+ wounded.
notableErrors: Iranian Red Crescent figures may undercount (government pressure) or overcount (propaganda value).
notes: Best available casualty source but Iranian Red Crescent operates under government influence. ICRC international figures more reliable.

name: UNICEF
bias: Child welfare mandate. Neutral.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Reported 181 children killed.
notableScoops: Child casualty tracking.
notes: UN agency. High credibility for child casualty figures.

name: WHO
bias: Health mandate. Neutral.
trackRecord: 6 mentions. Reported 13 medical facilities damaged/destroyed.
notableScoops: Medical facility tracking.
notes: UN agency. Essential for medical infrastructure damage tracking.

name: OCHA
bias: Humanitarian mandate. Neutral.
trackRecord: 2 mentions. Used for displacement and humanitarian situation.
notes: UN humanitarian coordination office.

name: Planet Labs
bias: None — satellite imagery.
trackRecord: 1 mention. Noted: 96-hour delay on Gulf satellite imagery (significant intelligence gap).
notableScoops: Satellite imagery verification of strike damage.
notableErrors: 96-hour delay limits real-time utility.
notes: Commercial satellite imagery. Objective verification tool. Delay noted as significant gap.

name: Cirium
bias: None — aviation data.
trackRecord: 4 mentions. Primary source for flight cancellation data (23,000+ flights cancelled).
notableScoops: Aviation disruption tracking.
notes: Aviation analytics company. Objective data source.

name: QatarEnergy
bias: Qatari state energy company.
trackRecord: 8 mentions. LNG halt announcements. $150/bbl forecast. Force majeure declarations.
notableScoops: First major LNG halt. $150/bbl price forecast.
notes: State energy company but market-facing statements tend to be accurate due to financial/legal implications.

name: OSINT Community
bias: Variable. Individual analysts have known biases. Pro-Ukraine OSINT community tends to be pro-Western.
trackRecord: 21 mentions. Used for real-time damage assessment, geolocated imagery, and flight tracking.
notableScoops: Real-time damage verification via satellite and social media.
notableErrors: Individual accounts vary widely in reliability. Misidentification of targets occurs.
notes: Collective term for open-source intelligence analysts on social media. Individual accounts must be evaluated separately. Valuable for speed but requires verification.

name: Telegram channels
bias: Highly variable. Many channels are fronts for state actors.
trackRecord: 5 mentions. Used for real-time reports from conflict zones, especially Iran (during internet blackout, limited Telegram access via VPN).
notableErrors: High disinformation risk. Unverified claims common.
notes: Primary social media platform for Middle East conflict reporting. Many channels are state-operated. Requires heavy verification.
